data.title: M/I Homes signals 5% community count growth in 2026 while navigating margin pressures
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: M/I Homes, Inc. (MHO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Schottenstein began by highlighting the company's 50th anniversary and stated, "we are the best financial condition in our history, have a group of leadership teams that are as strong as we've ever had and that we are well positioned in our 17 markets." Schottenstein reported, "Despite choppy demand, affordability challenges, economic uncertainty and other macroeconomic pressures, our performance remained very solid." He noted a 9% year-over-year increase in monthly new contracts during the fourth quarter and a 6% rise in average community count versus guidance of about 5%.
  • Schottenstein detailed 2025 results: 8,921 homes delivered, $4.4 billion in revenue, and pretax income of nearly $590 million excluding $59 million in charges. Gross margins, excluding charges, were 24.4%, down 220 basis points year-over-year, primarily due to higher incentives and lot costs. The Smart Series accounted for 49% of Q4 sales.
  • CFO Phillip Creek shared, "Our new contracts, were up 18% in October, a 9% -- excuse me, up 6% in November and up 4% in December for a 9% improvement in the quarter compared to last year's fourth quarter." Creek reported a fourth quarter sales pace of 2.8% and a cancellation rate of 10%. He added, "Revenue decreased 5% in the fourth quarter of 2025 to $1.1 billion."
  • Derek Klutch, President & CEO of M/I Financial, stated, "In the fourth quarter, our mortgage and title operations achieved pretax income of $8.5 million, down $1.6 million from 2024, and revenue of $27.8 million, down 20% from last year, primarily as a result of lower margins on loans closed and sold and partially offset by higher average loan amounts and more loans closed."

Outlook

  • Creek indicated, "We currently estimate that our average 2026 community count will be about 5% higher than 2025." Management expects a 5% increase in community count but did not provide guidance on margins for the next quarter.
  • Schottenstein said, "Despite the current challenging conditions, we feel very good about our business remain very confident in the long-term fundamentals of our industry and are well positioned as we begin 2026."

Financial Results

  • M/I Homes delivered 2,301 homes in Q4. Revenue for Q4 was $1.1 billion, a 5% decrease from the prior year. Average closing price dropped 1% to $484,000. Gross margin was 18.1% including $51 million in charges; excluding these, gross margin was 22.6%.
  • SG&A expense was flat year-over-year at 11.6% of revenue. The company generated $129 million of EBITDA for Q4 and $608 million for the year. EPS for the quarter was $2.39, down from $4.71 in the prior year’s Q4.
  • Share repurchases totaled $50 million in Q4 and $200 million for the year, with $220 million remaining under repurchase authority.

Q&A

  • Kenneth Zener, Seaport Research Partners, asked about Southern region growth. Creek responded, "In general, we had pretty solid sales everywhere. Our Carolina markets, Charlotte and Raleigh have done very well. In Florida, our Orlando market has actually held up pretty well, and Tampa also has improved as we've gone through the quarter."
  • Zener asked about spec vs. backlog margins and Smart Series contribution. Schottenstein explained, "the majority of 60% to 75% of the closings quarter-to-quarter-to-quarter, all coming from spec sales...Spec margins in general are lower than to-be-built homes."
  • Alan Ratner, Zelman, questioned order strength and incentives. Schottenstein replied, "I feel like demand is slightly picking up...It feels like we may be starting to see a slight improvement in demand. And I also think...the margins are likely to remain under pressure, but it's not clear to me at this point that the pressure in '26 will be as much as it was in '25."
  • Ratner inquired about impairments. Creek noted, "We always have a couple of problem subdivisions. Our impairment covered about 1,000 lots...it was in the most affordable stuff."
  • Buck Horne, Raymond James, asked about land spend. Creek responded, "We're continuing to do a lot of land development. We self-develop about 80% of our own land."
  • Horne also explored Florida trends and mortgage buydowns. Schottenstein said, "Orlando clearly held up the best...Tampa business has picked up very importantly." Klutch added, "We've been going with a 4, 7/8, 30-year fixed...getting a sub-5 is the key, and that's what really seems to attract the buyers."
  • Alex Barrón, Housing Research Center, and Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP, asked about margin outlook and spec inventory. Creek said, "We did deal with problem, communities that thought we needed to with the impairments don't give any guidance."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, probing deeply into margin pressures, incentives, and inventory strategies, while also recognizing the company’s ability to navigate a challenging environment.
  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks, with Schottenstein stating, "we are in the best financial condition in our 50-year history." During Q&A, management acknowledged ongoing pressures but maintained a constructive and transparent stance, as seen in, "Spec margins in general are lower than to-be-built homes."
  • Compared to last quarter, management demonstrated similar confidence, but with increased emphasis on flexibility and proactive adjustments in response to market demands.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for community count growth in 2026 was reiterated at 5%, consistent with prior quarter indications.
  • Margin pressure increased, with gross margin dropping to 18.1% (including charges) from the previous quarter’s 23.9%.
  • The Smart Series share of sales slipped from 52% to 49% in Q4.
  • Analysts continued to focus on regional performance, margin trends, incentives, and inventory management, while management highlighted improved contract trends and execution.
  • Management tone remained steady, but with more detailed discussion on flexibility and market adaptation strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management specifically cited continued affordability challenges, economic uncertainty, and margin pressures due to higher incentives and lot costs.
  • Inventory and warranty charges, particularly in entry-level communities, remain a challenge.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of demand, margin outlook, and the impact of spec-heavy sales on profitability.

Final Takeaway

M/I Homes management emphasized strong financial positioning entering 2026, with a projected 5% increase in average community count and robust liquidity. Despite ongoing affordability and margin pressures, the company remains focused on disciplined inventory management, strategic use of incentives, and maintaining flexibility in product offerings to adapt to evolving market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Otis signals mid- to high single-digit EPS growth for 2026 as modernization backlog surges 30%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Otis Worldwide Corporation (OTIS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Judith Marks, Chair, President & CEO, highlighted Otis’ achievement of record modernization orders, stating the company ended the year with "record adjusted free cash flow of $817 million in the fourth quarter" and a "modernization backlog up 30% at constant currency, the highest since spin and positioning us well for 2026." She emphasized strong execution of the uplift program, completion of China transformation initiatives, and growth of the largest maintenance portfolio in the industry, now at approximately 2.5 million units.
  • Marks reported, "We delivered double-digit adjusted EPS growth in the quarter, up 11%, which was the highest level this year and our strongest performance in the last 6 quarters." She added that subscription revenue from Otis ONE connected units increased 35% in 2025.
  • Cristina Mendez, Executive VP & CFO, noted, "Service operating profit of $638 million increased $49 million at constant currency," and that operating profit margins expanded 100 basis points to 25.5% in the quarter, matching record service margins from last quarter. Mendez also referenced the China modernization market, remarking that Otis’ "mod revenue in China... grew 100% in the fourth quarter year-over-year and was up 75% full year."

Outlook

  • Marks described a positive market outlook for 2026, expecting "the global new equipment market outlook to continue moving towards stabilization," with growth in the Americas and EMEA, acceleration in Asia Pacific, and moderating decline in China. She projected "total organic sales are expected to increase low to mid-single digits, driven by accelerating growth in our Service segment as well as moderating declines in new equipment sales."
  • For 2026, Otis expects "adjusted EPS to grow mid- to high single digits for the full year." Mendez specified, "At constant currency, adjusted operating profit is expected to grow $60 million to $100 million," and "adjusted free cash flow of $1.6 billion to $1.7 billion this year."
  • The company plans to "execute approximately $800 million share repurchases" and maintain a dividend payout ratio of 40%, while remaining open to bolt-on acquisitions.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net sales reached $3.8 billion, with organic sales up 1%. Service organic sales grew 5%, and modernization organic sales increased 9%.
  • Adjusted operating profit margin expanded 70 basis points to 16.6%, driven by a 100 basis point increase in service margin.
  • Adjusted EPS grew approximately 11% or $0.10 in the quarter, attributed to strong operational performance, favorable foreign exchange rates, and a lower share count.
  • New Equipment organic sales declined 6% in the quarter. EMEA sales grew 6%, Asia Pacific grew low single digits, while the Americas declined 5% and China experienced declines as well.
  • Modernization closed the year with record orders, with the backlog up 30% at constant currency.

Q&A

  • Amit Mehrotra, UBS: Asked about growth expectations for maintenance and repair in 2026, service profit trends, and retention and churn. Marks replied, "we are expecting our repair rates to ramp up to be 10% plus... at least a point gain in maintenance as well." Mendez stated, "we expect an acceleration of service contribution in the year... $200 million" in profit growth. Marks added, "Our goal in '25 was to stabilize that retention rate after seeing a decline from '24 to '25. And ex China, we're pleased to say that we've done that."
  • Joseph O'Dea, Wells Fargo: Inquired about service margin expansion and the impact of modernization mix. Mendez explained, "repair accelerated and repair has the highest margin within the Service segment. Modernization margins are ramping up... we increased 50 basis point modernization margin quarter-over-quarter."
  • Nicholas Housden, RBC: Asked about the annual growth potential in modernization. Marks indicated the market segment grew 13% in 2025 and suggested planning for "steady growth rate in the market... in the teens, if not more."
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research: Asked for specifics on China expectations for 2026 and margin dynamics. Marks projected the China market will be "about 8% down" in 2026, with the first half more negative and stabilization in the back half. Mendez confirmed China remains the highest margin region, but service margins are expanding due to modernization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of service growth, margin expansion, retention rates, the China outlook, and modernization growth, with a tone ranging from constructive to cautiously probing. Concerns over EPS guidance and the sustainability of margin expansion were noted.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently reinforcing operational execution, strategic investments, and the durability of its service flywheel. Marks stated, "We are confident that our strategy will continue to deliver attractive results in 2026 and beyond."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was slightly more optimistic about stabilization in new equipment and accelerating service momentum, but maintained a conservative stance in EPS guidance, as expressed by Marks: "we chose to be conservative."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to a more explicit expectation of mid- to high single-digit adjusted EPS growth, supported by a record modernization backlog.
  • Strategic focus intensified on modernization and service, with management highlighting industrialization of modernization offerings and targeting higher-value service units.
  • Analysts continued to emphasize retention rates, margin sustainability, and the modernization cycle, while management provided more detail on China’s evolving market and service portfolio composition.
  • Key metrics improved in service and modernization, while new equipment remained challenged but showed signs of stabilization.
  • Management reiterated confidence in service-driven growth, but reinforced disciplined, conservative guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "wage inflation, mix and churn" as partial offsets to margin gains.
  • New Equipment segment faces ongoing volume decline, especially from China, and competitive pricing pressure.
  • China market projected to decline "about 8%" in 2026, with ongoing structural challenges in contract renewals and competitive churn.
  • Commodities pose "small headwinds" for New Equipment margins, and the run rate from China transformation is expected to be "EUR 20 million smaller in the year-on-year basis than in 2025."
  • Analysts probed on the sustainability of service margin expansion and the impact of investments in service excellence on profitability.

Final Takeaway

Otis management emphasized record modernization orders and an expanding service portfolio as key drivers for anticipated mid- to high single-digit adjusted EPS growth in 2026. With a robust backlog and continued investments in service excellence and digital innovation, the company projects accelerating top line and operational performance, while maintaining a conservative guidance approach amid ongoing challenges in new equipment and the China market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AT&T outlines plan to reach 40M fiber locations by end of 2026 while projecting $45B+ in shareholder returns
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AT&T Inc. (T) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Stankey opened by stating, "We met or exceeded all of our consolidated full year financial guidance, driven by another solid year of 5G and fiber subscriber growth." He highlighted over 1.5 million postpaid phone net adds for the fifth consecutive year and over 1 million AT&T Fiber net adds for the eighth consecutive year. Stankey also noted, "We returned over $12 billion to our shareholders through dividends and buybacks, which was more than a 50% increase from 2024."
  • Stankey announced the closing of agreements to acquire spectrum licenses from EchoStar and fiber assets from Lumen, which are expected to close early in 2026. He said, "Including Gigapower and the fiber assets that we're acquiring from Lumen, we expect to reach over 40 million customer locations with our fiber services by the end of this year, up from 32 million at the end of 2025."
  • He emphasized a multi-year acceleration in fiber deployment, planning to expand fiber reach by approximately 5 million locations annually through the end of the decade. Stankey stated, "Our fiber convergence rate climbed 200 basis points year-over-year to 42%, which is our fastest annual increase since we began tracking this metric."
  • The CEO outlined a new segment reporting structure beginning in Q1 2026, separating "advanced connectivity" (domestic 5G and fiber) from legacy operations.
  • CFO Pascal Desroches said, "We achieved over 4% growth in consolidated adjusted EBITDA during the fourth quarter, while expanding adjusted EBITDA margins by 20 basis points." Desroches also stated, "Adjusted EPS grew by over 20% in the fourth quarter to $0.52 and nearly 9% for the year to $2.12."

Outlook

  • AT&T expects total wireless service revenue growth in the 2% to 3% range annually over the next three years and organic advanced home Internet service revenue growth of 20% plus annually through 2028. Following the Lumen acquisition, reported advanced home Internet revenue growth in 2026 is expected to exceed 30%.
  • Desroches said, "We expect adjusted EPS to be in the $2.25 to $2.35 range in 2026 with a double-digit 3-year CAGR through 2028." Free cash flow is projected at $18 billion plus in 2026, growing by $1 billion plus in 2027 and approximately $2 billion in 2028.
  • The company plans to return $45 billion plus to shareholders from 2026 to 2028, including about $8 billion in buybacks in 2026.

Financial Results

  • AT&T reported over $1 billion in cost savings for 2025, with a plan to achieve an additional $4 billion in annual cost savings by the end of 2028. Full-year free cash flow was $16.6 billion.
  • Net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ended at 2.53x, with cash and cash equivalents of $18.2 billion. The company closed on a $17.5 billion delayed draw term facility during the quarter. Desroches noted, "Immediately following the closing of these transactions, we expect our net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA to increase to approximately 3.2x and then to decline to approximately 3x by year-end as we grow adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow."

Q&A

  • John Hodulik, UBS: Asked about future convergence rate targets and Lumen territory performance. Stankey responded, "We have an objective...to drive that to 50% and feel very comfortable with that. I don't expect it to stop there." He also discussed a cautious approach to Lumen, stating if penetration matches in-region performance, "that's going to be upside in terms of our business case."
  • Benjamin Swinburne, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about spectrum investment capacity and the impact of a foldable iPhone. Stankey said, "We have reserved capacity for other strategic options," and suggested that device form factor shifts are unlikely to drive material changes in customer demand.
  • Peter Supino, Wolfe Research: Questioned fiber ARPU and FWA growth as DSL declines. Stankey emphasized, "We currently sit under their (cable) pricing umbrella...we have a lot more degrees of freedom in how we manage our ARPUs." He projected continued DSL decline by design, with no supply-demand concern for broadband.
  • Michael Rollins, Citi: Asked about macro drivers of postpaid phone growth and responses to competitor promotions. Jeffery McElfresh, COO, referenced opportunities in underpenetrated segments and convergence strategy.
  • Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan: Sought clarity on leverage targets and seasonality in fiber build pace. Desroches confirmed expectations for equity partner proceeds, while Stankey projected ongoing seasonality, with ramping from the Lumen integration affecting early quarters.
  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs: Requested details on EBITDA growth inflection and cost initiatives. Desroches explained that fewer legacy contributions and greater penetration of new assets would drive acceleration by 2028.
  • Samuel McHugh, BNP: Queried bad debt trends and cost cutting. Desroches attributed higher bad debt to increased equipment sales, not consumer payment issues, and highlighted $4 billion in expected cost savings over three years.
  • Michael Funk, BofA: Asked about marketing strategy for FWA and ARPU headwinds. Stankey explained a broad national message, supplemented by targeted offers, and Desroches reiterated a disciplined investment focus on converged relationships.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised nuanced questions focused on convergence, ARPU, and integration execution, with a tone that was neutral to slightly positive but pressed for specifics on guidance and cost management.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Stankey stated, "I'm confident the teams demonstrated that they can be pretty fluid and creative in how they approach these things." During Q&A, the tone remained optimistic but acknowledged operational challenges and seasonality with statements like "we're going to be on a learning curve on some of that stuff."
  • Compared to last quarter, analysts' tone was more focused on integration and guidance clarity, while management’s confidence remained steady, with added emphasis on execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from reiterating targets to providing multi-year projections through 2028, including new segment reporting for advanced connectivity.
  • Strategic focus expanded from organic and acquisition-driven growth to a detailed plan for fiber expansion and cost transformation, with explicit targets for convergence rates and cash returns.
  • Key metrics saw notable increases, such as a 20%+ adjusted EPS growth in Q4 and a jump in fiber passings target from 32 million to 40 million by year-end 2026.
  • Management tone remained confident, but now included more details on operational ramp and integration, while analysts shifted from broad strategy to specifics on execution and cost discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged integration challenges with the Lumen assets and the need for a "solid 2-quarter ramp" to optimize operations and distribution.
  • Seasonality in broadband net additions and the impact of promotional dynamics on ARPU were highlighted.
  • Analysts questioned the company’s ability to manage bad debt and maintain cost savings momentum; management attributed bad debt increases to higher equipment sales and not consumer distress.
  • Upfront investments required for integration and fiber deployment are expected to impact Q1 results.

Final Takeaway

AT&T’s leadership emphasized strong execution in 2025, achieving or exceeding all guidance targets and accelerating its long-term growth strategy. The company projects reaching over 40 million fiber locations by the end of 2026 and returning $45 billion plus to shareholders over the next three years, supported by growth in its advanced connectivity segment and cost transformation initiatives. Management remains confident in its ability to drive profitable growth, manage integration of new assets, and deliver enhanced shareholder returns through disciplined execution and expanding customer relationships.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Merck veteran-led Eikon seeks $908M valuation in U.S. IPO ‍
data.description:
Eikon ‍Therapeutics (EIKN), a cancer drug developer led by Merck (MRK) veteran Roger Perlmutter, has filed for its U.S. IPO seeking a valuation of up to $908M with roughly 17.6M shares on offer.

The Millbrae, California-based biotech is planning to raise about $273.5M in net proceeds (nearly $315.4M including the underwriters’ option to purchase additional shares), assuming the midpoint of its marketed price range of $16 - $18 per share.

The company, which intends to list on Nasdaq under the ticker symbol “EIKN,” can command a valuation of up to $908.2M at the top of the range, given the roughly 50.5M shares expected to be outstanding immediately after the offering.

Eikon is led by CEO Perlmutter, a former president of Merck Research Laboratories (MRK), and several other former top brass of the pharma giant, including medical chief Roy Baynes and board member Ken Frazier.

Being a clinical-stage biotechnology company, Eikon (EIKN) has incurred sizable losses since its launch in 2019.

And its pipeline is led by EIK1001, an experimental medicine licensed from Edison, New Jersey-based Seven and Eight Biotherapeutics. EIKN’s near-term catalysts include initial data expected in H2 2026 from a global Phase 2/3 registrational trial for EIK1001 in advanced malignant melanoma.

data.title: Federal Reserve hits pause, after three-meeting cutting streak
data.description:
The Federal Reserve's Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate at 3.50%-3.75% on Wednesday, as widely expected, pausing the easing implemented at its three previous meetings.

The recent series of cuts lowered the federal fund rate target range by 75 basis points in the past four months of 2025 from the 4.25%-4.50% level it held for the first eight months of the year.

Two members of the FOMC dissented from the majority. Stephen I. Miran and Christopher J. Waller preferred to cut the target rang by 25 basis points at Wednesday's meeting.

Developing… Check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: AES Corp. gains amid renewed takeover speculation
data.description:
• AES Corp. (AES) ticked higher by 1.3% amid some renewed takeover speculation for the power producer. 
  • Talks between AES (AES) and its potential suitors may have advanced, according to traders, who cited a Betaville "uncooked" alert that was circulating on Wednesday. It's not clear which company has advanced talks with AES.
  • There were reports last year that BlackRock's (BLK) Global Infrastructure Partners LP and Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) were both interested in buying AES (AES).

data.title: National Bank Holdings outlines 10% loan growth and $4 EPS target for 2027 as Vista integration advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: National Bank Holdings Corporation (NBHC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Tim Laney welcomed Vista teammates, highlighting the swift closure of the Vista Bank acquisition and emphasizing, "While the fourth quarter was noisy, we ended the year having grown tangible book per share by 10%, and we grew our CET1 capital ratio to 14.89%." He stated, "Our goal was to enter 2026 with a clean slate and with a focus on profitable growth."
  • Laney praised the completion of Phase 1 of the 2UniFi build, noting a strong net interest income margin of 3.97% to close the year.
  • CFO Nicole Van Denabeele described 2025 as a year where "we executed on key strategic priorities," specifically referencing the Vista acquisition, tangible book value growth, and a full-year net interest margin of 3.94%. She reported, "Excluding onetime items, fourth quarter net income totaled $22.7 million or $0.60 of earnings per diluted share."
  • Van Denabeele explained, "We kick off the year with a combined loan portfolio of approximately $9.4 billion and are projecting 2026 loan growth to be approximately 10%."
  • John Steinmetz, representing Vista, expressed, "We have partnered with a dynamic, high-performing team and platform, and I believe there is a tremendous potential for our combined organization."
  • President Aldis Birkans highlighted, "We originated $591 million in total loans, the second highest loan production quarter in our company's history. $429 million of that came from commercial loan originations, a new record for us."

Outlook

  • NBHC projects 2026 loan growth of approximately 10%, with combined loan portfolio starting at $9.4 billion.
  • Management expects the combined bank to generate earning asset growth of 7% to 10% during 2026.
  • The company projects a fully taxable equivalent net interest margin for 2026 to "remain right around 4%, excluding the impact of future rate moves."
  • For 2026, noninterest income is projected in the range of $75 million to $80 million, and noninterest expense between $320 million and $330 million, with the first half expected at $165 million to $170 million.
  • The company expects to deliver "earnings in excess of $1 per share in the fourth quarter of 2026, which sets the stage for full year earnings exceeding $4 per share in 2027."
  • 2UniFi revenue contribution for 2026 is expected to be $2 million to $4 million, with expenses held flat at $22 million.
  • Effective tax rate for 2026 is projected at approximately 20%.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net income, excluding onetime items, was $22.7 million or $0.60 per diluted share.
  • On an adjusted basis, full year 2025 net income was $117.6 million or $3.06 per diluted share.
  • Return on tangible assets was 1.3% and return on tangible common equity was 12.2% for 2025.
  • Fourth quarter noninterest income was $14.4 million, including $3.3 million in pretax securities losses.
  • Fourth quarter noninterest expense totaled $72.4 million, including $5.4 million of acquisition costs.
  • Allowance to total loans ratio ended the year at 1.18%.
  • CET1 ratio ended the year at 14.9%.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Confirmed 10% loan growth, margin near 4%, earnings over $1 in Q4 2026, and $4 in 2027; Nicole Van Denabeele: "That is correct."
  • Rulis: Asked about 2UniFi revenue and costs; Van Denabeele: "$2 million to $4 million 2UniFi revenue projection for 2026, and we will be holding 2UniFi expense flat in 2026, consistent with 2025, which was $22 million."
  • Rulis: Inquired about potential partnership to reduce 2UniFi costs; Tim Laney: "We are very focused on working to establish a partnership that frankly could have the effect, amongst other options, of moving this off the NBH financials altogether..."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about growth drivers for 2026; Aldis Birkans and John Steinmetz pointed to contributions from all markets, with strong momentum in commercial loans and expansion in Texas and resort markets.
  • Motta: Questioned margin dynamics and deposit repricing; Van Denabeele: December margin was "a strong 3.97%... There wasn't any interest reversals in the fourth quarter, and the loans that we worked through were already on nonaccrual."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Asked about partnership timing for 2UniFi; Laney acknowledged previous optimism was premature but emphasized focus on "bringing the right partnership together and moving 2UniFi ahead."
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde Group: Asked about payoff activity and outlook for net loan growth; Laney and Birkans described diminished scheduled maturities and a risk-off stance shifting to higher confidence for 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management for specifics on growth, margin, and 2UniFi partnership timing, showing a cautiously optimistic but probing tone.
  • Management presented a confident outlook, with Van Denabeele and Laney repeatedly affirming guidance and strategic direction, using phrases like "we are confident" and "we are well positioned."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone is more assertive about growth and integration benefits, while analysts maintain a consistent focus on execution and future performance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is now explicit, with 10% loan growth and full-year earnings targets, compared to more cautious or preliminary statements in Q3.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from announcing the Vista acquisition to emphasizing its integration and the benefits for growth, especially in Texas and resort markets.
  • Net interest margin guidance remains in the mid-3.9% to 4% range, with management addressing margin drivers more directly than in Q3.
  • Expense guidance is more detailed, reflecting Vista integration and 2UniFi's shift to operating phase.
  • Analysts' questions continue to focus on growth, margin sustainability, and technology investments, but with greater attention to integration execution and profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited elevated provision expense and onetime acquisition costs as fourth-quarter headwinds.
  • The sale of investment securities to avoid surpassing $10 billion in assets was executed to preserve interchange income, but resulted in a $2.6 million after-tax loss.
  • Problem loans were proactively addressed, with $9.1 million in provision expense; Laney stated, "The decision -- we believe a prudent decision was to address these as aggressively as we could in '25 and have a clean runway for '26."
  • Analysts voiced concerns about loan payoffs, credit quality, and the impact of private credit competition, but management expressed confidence in improved asset quality and diminished headwinds for 2026.

Final Takeaway

National Bank Holdings Corporation closed 2025 with the successful integration of Vista Bank, a robust capital position, and a clear focus on profitable growth for 2026. Management projects strong momentum with 10% loan growth, a stable 4% net interest margin, and the launch of 2UniFi's next phase. The company targets over $1 per share in earnings for the fourth quarter of 2026, setting the stage for full-year earnings above $4 per share in 2027, while proactively managing credit, integration costs, and operational efficiency.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PPG targets mid-single-digit EPS growth in 2026 amid aerospace momentum and operational cost reductions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PPG Industries (PPG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Timothy Knavish reported that “2025 was a year of solid commercial, operational, innovation and financial performance for PPG,” highlighting consistent organic growth and strong cash flow, despite a dynamic macroeconomic environment. Knavish emphasized that net sales for the year reached $15.9 billion with 2% organic growth, and adjusted earnings per share were $7.58.
  • Knavish noted, “We achieved record aerospace coating sales and earnings led by strong demand for our technology advanced products,” and cited a 6% increase in Auto OEM net sales, which outpaced the industry. He also pointed to high single-digit organic sales growth in Architectural Coatings in Latin America and broad-based positive sales volume across all regions.
  • Knavish shared that “our segment EBITDA margin for the year was a healthy 19%,” and cash from operations grew to $1.9 billion, enabling $1.4 billion to be returned to shareholders.
  • Knavish addressed capital deployment, stating, “We ended the year with a strong cash balance of $2.2 billion and a net debt position of $5.1 billion, with $700 million of debt maturing in the first quarter of 2026, which we intend to pay from our current cash position.”
  • Senior VP & CFO Vincent Morales stated, “our segment earnings did grow in the fourth quarter. So they were up about $20 million year-over-year.”

Outlook

  • Knavish provided 2026 guidance, anticipating “organic sales growth in the range of flat to positive low single-digit percentage” and “earnings per share growth that at the midpoint of our guidance represents a mid-single-digit percentage increase.” He added, “We expect earnings per share to be flat to growth of low single-digit percentage during the first half of the year and increasing to high single-digit percentage in the second half of the year.”
  • Management expects positive pricing in most performance and architectural businesses, with some offset from modest price declines in the industrial segment. Knavish commented, “You should expect to see positive price in virtually all of the protective businesses... and the Architectural businesses.”
  • Raw material costs are expected to be flat for the year, with some inflation in epoxies, specialty pigments, and metal packaging offset by softness in TiO2 and solvents.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net sales were $3.9 billion, up 5% year-over-year with 3% organic growth. Adjusted EPS for the quarter was $1.51. Segment EBITDA margin for the quarter was 18%.
  • Global Architectural Coatings net sales rose 8% to $951 million, driven by Mexico’s retail performance and project-related recovery. Segment income increased 16% and EBITDA margins improved by nearly 100 basis points.
  • Performance Coatings delivered 5% net sales growth to $1.3 billion, led by double-digit organic growth in aerospace and gains in Protective & Marine Coatings, offset by high single-digit declines in Automotive Refinish organic sales.
  • Industrial Coatings net sales grew 3% to $1.6 billion, with 5% sales volume growth propelled by share gains, and EBITDA margin improved to 15.1%.
  • Operating cash flow for the year was $1.9 billion, supporting share repurchases of $790 million and dividends of $630 million.

Q&A

  • Christopher Parkinson, Wolfe Research: Asked about the drivers of Q4 growth and 2026 expectations. Knavish: “The high-level answer is macro is not better than we expect…there’s a few markets where we’re getting macro help. Most of our businesses were getting share gain help. And…a lot of the share that we’re gaining in packaging is technology driven.”
  • Aleksey Yefremov, KeyBanc: Requested details on organic growth drivers for 2026. Knavish explained positive price trends in most segments and volume growth in aerospace, PMC, packaging, and auto OEM.
  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research: Questioned flat EBITDA despite organic sales growth. Knavish cited “Refinish destocking” as the main drag, with improvement expected in the second half of 2026. Morales added, “our segment earnings did grow in the fourth quarter…up about $20 million year-over-year.”
  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: Asked for updates on Refinish. Knavish reiterated confidence in market share momentum, with recovery expected in the second half, supported by new digital and AI tools.
  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about aerospace growth and capacity. Knavish: “The growth rate for 2025 was double digit…we’re guiding high single digits for 2026 for Aerospace. We are capacity constrained.”
  • Multiple analysts probed on capital allocation, corporate costs, and M&A; Knavish and Morales highlighted continued buybacks, disciplined capital investment, and prioritization of organic growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone reflected cautious optimism but focused on macro uncertainty, operating leverage challenges, and the impact of Refinish destocking. Questions repeatedly pressed for clarity on recovery timing and margin progression.
  • Management maintained confidence during prepared remarks, emphasizing share gains, innovation, and disciplined cost control. In Q&A, Knavish acknowledged ongoing challenges but reinforced, “We’re confident in our strategy and the strength of our business model to deliver sustainable long-term growth.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained slightly negative regarding near-term earnings leverage, while management’s tone was steady and focused on execution and operational resilience.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to a more cautious outlook for the first half of 2026, with explicit expectation of “flat to low single-digit EPS growth” before improving in the second half. This contrasts with the prior quarter’s outlook, which anticipated organic sales and earnings growth but flagged transitory headwinds.
  • Strategic focus remains on organic growth, operational efficiency, and innovation, particularly in aerospace and digital/AI development. Portfolio pruning and cash deployment priorities are consistent.
  • Analysts maintained focus on Refinish normalization, aerospace capacity, and regional performance trends, echoing concerns seen last quarter but seeking more granular recovery signals.
  • Key metric changes include a rise in cash from operations, improved segment EBITDA margins, and continued share repurchases.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited expected softness in global industrial and automotive demand affecting Q1 2026 organic sales and margins.
  • Refinish destocking and normalization of distributor buying patterns are identified as major drags in the first half of 2026, with recovery expected in the second half.
  • Exposure to tariffs, especially in epoxies and specialty pigments, and potential volatility in project-related sales in Mexico and Europe are noted.
  • Analysts highlighted the challenge of achieving operating leverage given margin pressure and corporate cost inflation.

Final Takeaway

PPG’s management highlighted the company’s resilience and multi-engine growth strategy, with 2025 marked by record aerospace sales, strong cash generation, and steady share gains. The outlook for 2026 anticipates muted first-half performance driven by continued Refinish destocking and macro headwinds, but expects volume and margin expansion in the second half, supported by ongoing operational improvements, cost discipline, and innovation in high-growth segments. The management reiterated confidence in delivering sustainable growth and disciplined capital deployment, underpinned by a strong balance sheet and focused execution.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Badger Meter outlines 5-year high single-digit sales growth outlook as PRASA project ramps up
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Badger Meter (BMI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kenneth Bockhorst, Chairman, President & CEO, stated the company "delivered solid financial results in the fourth quarter, capping off another full year of record sales, profitability and cash flow." He highlighted robust demand for the cellular AMI solution and the integration of SmartCover into the BlueEdge suite of smart water management solutions. Bockhorst also noted the recent award for the PRASA AMI project in Puerto Rico as positioning the company well for long-term growth.
  • Daniel Weltzien, newly appointed VP, CFO & Treasurer, said, "Total sales of $221 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 represented an increase of 8% year-over-year or 2% base sales growth." Weltzien added, "Operating profit margins increased 40 basis points from 19.1% to 19.5%. Base operating earnings increased 9% year-over-year, expanding base operating profit margins by 140 basis points to 20.5%. Gross margins expanded 180 basis points to 42.1% in the fourth quarter from 40.3% in the prior year quarter."
  • Robert Wrocklage, Executive Vice President of North America Municipal Utility, described the PRASA AMI project as "one of the largest deployments in the world," involving 1.6 million service connections and utilizing E-Series ultrasonic meters, ORION Cellular AMI radios, and BEACON SaaS. He emphasized that Badger Meter's role will be supply only and that production will be based in Racine, Wisconsin.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a high single-digit sales growth outlook over the next five years. Bockhorst stated, "We expect this project pacing dynamic to extend throughout the first half of 2026 until several awarded projects, including PRASA, begin multiyear turnkey deployments."
  • The company does not plan to size the revenue impact of the PRASA project for 2026 or beyond, due to variables affecting deployment timing. Bockhorst emphasized, "We are confident... to generate high single-digit sales growth, operating profit margin expansion and free cash flow conversion in excess of earnings over a 5-year forward time horizon."

Financial Results

  • The company reported total sales of $221 million for Q4 2025, up 8% year-over-year. Utility water product line sales increased 9% year-over-year. Operating earnings grew 10% year-over-year. EPS was $1.14 versus $1.04 in the prior year quarter.
  • Gross margins reached 42.1% in Q4 2025, compared to 40.3% in the previous year. Quarterly free cash flow was $50.8 million, an increase of approximately $3.4 million year-over-year.
  • Software revenue, which includes SmartCover, now exceeds $74 million and represents 8% of total sales. SmartCover delivered $40 million in sales in 2025.

Q&A

  • Robert Mason, Robert W. Baird: Asked about project timing and whether the full impact of project conclusions was seen in Q4. Bockhorst replied, "The first half started out with a higher growth rate, the second half of the year with a lower growth rate. I think the way we see '26 is a lower growth rate in the first half and a higher growth rate in the second half."
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Sought clarity on the size and relevance of the PRASA project. Bockhorst explained that PRASA is equivalent to "8 Orlandos" in scale and is highlighted for its scope.
  • Jones also inquired about gross margins. Weltzien responded that structural mix will continue to impact margins positively in 2026, while noting that commodity input costs and tariffs remain areas to monitor.
  • Jeffrey Reive, RBC: Asked about the timing and phasing of large AMI projects. Bockhorst said project ramp-ups are inherently variable and typically expected to unfold over a five-year horizon.
  • Andrew Krill, Deutsche Bank: Questioned sales outlook versus competitors. Bockhorst stated, "We feel confident sales should grow this year even if they're below that high single-digit through the cycle target."
  • Scott Graham, Seaport: Asked about the use of capital allocation to support growth and returns. Bockhorst asserted, "We would never do any shortsighted cost-cutting type things to try to short circuit a result."
  • Michael Fairbanks, JPMorgan: Asked about SmartCover growth prospects. Bockhorst noted, "We fully, fully expect to continue to grow our sewer line monitoring at a higher rate than average."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on project timing, margin sustainability, and the scale of new wins, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as questions sought clarification on timing and strategic growth rather than expressing skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, frequently referencing visibility into awarded projects and reiterating multi-year growth convictions. Phrases such as "I'm absolutely confident" and "our 5-year conviction is as strong as it's ever been" underscored this stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a similarly constructive and forward-looking tone, with management expressing slightly more confidence in awarded project pipelines and integration success.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for high single-digit sales growth over five years remains unchanged, but management provided more detail on project pacing, indicating a lower growth rate in the first half of 2026 and a higher rate in the second half, which is a shift from the previous quarter's higher first-half growth.
  • The PRASA project was discussed in greater depth, highlighting its scale and expected impact on the second half of 2026 and beyond.
  • Management tone remains confident, with increased emphasis on the recurring nature of project revenue and the durability of secular growth drivers.
  • SmartCover's integration progress and profitability improved, with software revenue and SaaS attachment highlighted as growing contributors.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing global tariff and trade uncertainties as a potential headwind for 2026, along with elevated copper and other component prices as a gross margin headwind.
  • There is acknowledged variability in project timing, with awarded projects sometimes sliding by a quarter or two. Weltzien noted, "Each one of these projects is different. Every customer is different."
  • The PRASA project's variables, including deployment pace and funding, could influence year-to-year revenue contributions.

Final Takeaway

Management reiterated its confidence in the company's ability to deliver high single-digit sales growth, operating margin expansion, and strong cash flow conversion over the next five years, supported by robust demand for smart water management solutions and a deep pipeline of awarded and prospective projects. The scale and timing of the PRASA project, continued SmartCover integration, and focus on capital allocation for strategic growth remain central themes for value creation in 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Logitech falls despite strong Q3 while Wedbush maintains bullish views
data.description:
Shares of Logitech (LOGI) fell about 7% on Wednesday despite fiscal third quarter results beating estimates.

Wedbush kept its Outperform rating and $135 price target on Logitech's stock. 

"Logitech has skillfully managed significant margin pressure through product innovation, cost reductions, targeted promotional activity, and supply chain adjustments, leading to gross and operating margin expansion YoY in FQ3. Meanwhile, Logitech has maintained focus on its core business initiatives – expanding B2B, market share in China, reaccelerating video conferencing, and leading in personal workspace solutions (“PWS”) across its markets," said analysts led by Alicia Reese.

The analysts noted that despite the focus on depressed PC shipments in calendar 2026 and potentially beyond due to high memory and other PC component costs, Logitech CEO Hanneke Faber shifted the focus to Logitech’s much larger opportunity to expand attach rates on theexisting PC base.

The analysts added that of the 1.5B to 2B PCs already owned and in use today, fewer than half have an upgraded mouse, and fewer than one-third have an upgraded  keyboard. This indicates the massive white space Logitech can tap into by increasing its marketing initiatives in this category where it has a dominant global market share.

"Logitech's impressive product updates across categories, effective inventory management, and a revitalized Chinese market keep us optimistic as the company plans for FY:27. Driven by its early rebound in video collaboration, expansion into new end markets, successful reinvigoration in China, and PWS strength, Logitech now has $12 per share in cash and has no debt, positioning it to accelerate growth through small tuck-in M&A, facilitate share repurchases, and expand its dividend," said Reese and her team.

The analysts noted that Logitech’s fiscal third quarter revenue of $1.422B exceeded their estimate of $1.415B and the consensus estimate of $1.406B.

Reese and her team noted that the fiscal fourth quarter outlook was above consensus with revenue guided between $1.07B and $1.09B (versus consensus of $1.08B) and operating income between $155M and $165M (versus consensus of $161M and our prior estimate of $175M).
